repo.description: The most widely used Python to C compiler
repo.name: cython

body:
This PR adds coercion and inference for `list[TYPE]`. It allows cython optimize following examples:

```python
a: list[str] = 'Foo'
b: list[str] = 'Bar'
print(a[0] + b[0])
```
The PR allows cython to optimise `a[0] + b[0]` to use `__Pyx_PyUnicode_Concat__Pyx_ReferenceSharing_OwnStrongReferenceInPlaceSafe()` instead of `PyNumber_Add()`

> [!NOTE]  
> The same effect can be done when code is rewritten as follows:
> ```python
> a: list[str] = 'Foo'
> b: list[str] = 'Bar'
> c: str = a[0]
> d: str = d[0]
> print(c + d)
> ``` 

PR also implements inference allowing to write efficiently loops:

```python
b: cython.int = 1
a: list[cython.int] = [2]
for c in a:
    print(b + c)
```

The example above infer type of `c` variable and declare `__pyx_v_c` as `int`. Hence the `b + c` line is generated as `__pyx_v_b + __pyx_v_c` instead of `PyNumber_Add()`

> [!NOTE]  
> The same effect can be done when code is rewritten as follows:
> ```python
> b: cython.int = 1
> a: list[cython.int] = [2]
> c: cython.int
> for c in a:
>     print(b + c)
> ``` 
> Also note that `infer_types` directive must be set to `True`


> [!CAUTION]
> ~~This PR is just for early feedback. The changes are just exploratory containing a lot of dirty workarounds.~~ Caveats:
> * ~~Only `list` is supported for now. It can be easilly extended to `set`. `dict` and `tuple` are more complex and maybe can be done in second round~~ Types `list`, `set`, `frozenset` and `dict` are supported. `tuple` is not in scope of this PR.
> ~~* PR supports coercion and inference for 1 level (`list[tuple]`) all higher nested levels (`list[list[int]]`) looses type information (they work correctly but the generated code is not optimised)~~


## TODO:
* [x] Make `get_container_type()` available for all Cython types (see https://github.com/cython/cython/pull/7288#pullrequestreview-3467280514)
* [x] implement casts like `list_long = <list[cython.long]> x` (or `cython.cast(…)`) to change the item type inference from C int to C long. Even `<list[cython.float]> x` seems a reasonable thing to do in that regard. (see https://github.com/cython/cython/pull/7288#issuecomment-3535132454)
* [x] add support recursive types (`list[list[...]`, `list[set[...]]`, `dict[int, list[...]]`, ...)
* [x] infer types also when container methods are used e.g. `.keys()`, `.items()`, ... (partially in #7424)
* [ ] ~~add support for tuples `tuple[cython.int, cython.float]` and `tuple[cython.int, ...]`~~ - this can be tricky to implement, I would postpone it to separate PR...
* [x] Fix https://github.com/cython/cython/pull/7288/files#diff-7f63ffd84e67d3d113d7ad10fa110111c779a8ca5edd7e12dc51097b36405eabR4904
* [x] improve tests to check `x: list[int] = tuple_of_int` - see  https://github.com/cython/cython/pull/7288#discussion_r2529316564
No

comment_data.body:
This fix is needed to fix failing test of `test_dataclasses`. The issue was strange. Cython complained about redeclared: `AssertionError: None != "272:15: 'list[User_TestCase_test_helper_asdict_builtin_containers]' redeclared"`

The issue surprisingly occurs **only** when both cpp and C tests are run at once (running the test is successful when `--no-c` or `--no-cpp` arguments are provided)

It seems that between the runs two different instances of `PyExtensionType` are created. This breaks following line: https://github.com/cython/cython/pull/7288/files#diff-7f63ffd84e67d3d113d7ad10fa110111c779a8ca5edd7e12dc51097b36405eabR4908

comment_data.body:
I think this is your problem for the dataclass issue.

You're essentially declaring `list[subscripted_type]` as an attribute of the main `list` builtin type (and the `list` builtin types are persistent when compiling multiple modules in sequence).

i.e. `self.scope` is the attributes of `list` (i.e. `append`, `extend`, `reverse` etc).

I'm not sure off the top of my head what the right scope to use is. But hopefully that helps

comment_data.body:
I generalised this check but still I am a little bit puzzled because this code seems does not validate the assignment. E.g. code
```python
def main():
    a: str = 'asdf'
    b: bytes = a
```
errors out with `Cannot convert Unicode string to 'bytes' implicitly, encoding required.` which clearly is coming from `find_coercion_error()`, for `list`:

```python
def main():
    a: list[str] = ['asdf']
    b: list[bytes] = a
```
errors out with `Cannot assign type 'list[str object] object' to 'list[bytes object] object'`

The error is correct in `list[...]` case but it is not coming from `find_coercion_error()`, so the check in that function is in reality not needed.

You don't need to implement both, Python will handle `!=` for you if `==` is implemented.
And the usual way to deal with unknown types in comparisons is to not handle them.
```suggestion
    def __eq__(self, other):
        if isinstance(other, BuiltinTypeConstructorObjectType):
            return self.get_container_type().name == other.get_container_type().name
        return NotImplemented
```

+            if template_values in self.specializations:
+                return self.specializations[template_values]
+            subscripted_types = ','.join([str(tv) for tv in template_values])
+            name = f'{self.get_container_type().name}[{subscripted_types}]'
+
+            # TODO this code is copied from Symtab.py, we need some common function for that...
+            objstruct_cname = 'PySetObject' if name == 'frozenset' else f'Py{name.capitalize()}Object'
+            typ = BuiltinTypeConstructorObjectType(name=name, cname=self.cname, objstruct_cname=objstruct_cname)
+            typ.base_type = self
+            typ.subscripted_types = tuple(template_values)
+            env.global_scope().declare_type(name, typ, pos, cname=typ.cname)
+            typ.scope = self.scope
+            self.specializations[template_values] = typ
+            return typ
+        # for a lot of the typing classes it doesn't really matter what the template is
+        # (i.e. typing.Dict[int] is really just a dict)
+        return self
+
+    def assignable_from(self, src_type):
+        if self == src_type:
+            if not self.subscripted_types and not src_type.subscripted_types:
+                return True
+            if not self.subscripted_types and src_type.subscripted_types:
+                return True
+            if self.subscripted_types and not src_type.subscripted_types:
+                return True
+            if all(dst_sc.assignable_from(src_sc) for dst_sc, src_sc in zip(self.subscripted_types, src_type.subscripted_types)):
+                return True
+            return False
+        return super().assignable_from(src_type)
+
+    def type_check_function(self, exact=True):
+        type_name = self.get_container_type().name
+        if type_name in _special_type_check_functions:
+            type_check = _special_type_check_functions[type_name]
+        elif self.is_exception_type:
+            type_check = f"__Pyx_PyExc_{type_name}_Check"
+        else:
+            type_check = f'Py{type_name.capitalize()}_Check'
+        if exact and not self.is_exception_type and type_name not in ('bool', 'slice', 'memoryview'):
+            type_check += 'Exact'
+        return type_check
+
+    def infer_indexed_type(self):
+        from .Builtin import dict_type
+        if self.get_container_type() == dict_type:
+            return self.get_subscripted_type(1)
+        else:
+            return self.get_subscripted_type(0)
+
+    def infer_iterator_type(self):
+        return self.get_subscripted_type(0)
+
+    def get_container_type(self):
+        """Returns the basic container type of the (potentially subscripted) type,
+        or None if not a container type.
+
+        Similar to typing.get_origin().
+        """
+        base_type = self.base_type
+        if base_type:
+            return base_type.get_container_type()
+        return self
+
+    def __eq__(self, other):
+        return isinstance(other, BuiltinTypeConstructorObjectType) and self.get_container_type().name == other.get_container_type().name
+
+    def __ne__(self, other):
+        return not (isinstance(other, BuiltinTypeConstructorObjectType) and self.get_container_type().name == other.get_container_type().name)

i.e. -> Latin "id est" == English "that is" or "meaning: …"

Hmm if you mean to replace:

            if not self.subscripted_types and not src_type.subscripted_types:

            if self.subscripted_types == not src_type.subscripted_types:

    def __eq__(self, other):

comment_data.body:
The docstring seems to contradict the declared signature return type and/or the implementation.

Also, the usages seem to assume that this never returns `None`. I don't think that should be the case. If the type is not a container, or if it cannot support item types, it seems reasonable that this returns `None`. Otherwise, simple name comparisons (like `self.get_container_type().name == other.get_container_type().name`) seem unsafe because users might achieve to define a non-builtin type under the name of a builtin type, and that should not compare equal to a known builtin type.

I think we can assign these statically at object initialisation time, even with a bit of `getattr()` magic like
```python
    def __init__(self, …):
        container_type = self.get_container_type()
        if container_type:
            type_attr_name = f"is_{container_type]_type"
            assert getattr(self, type_attr_name) == False, (self, type_attr_name)  # safety check
            setattr(self, type_attr_name, True)
```

        elif (self.lhs.type in Builtin.typed_container_types and

comment_data.body: Yeah this is a little workaround. I put there a comment hoping that it will clarify it a little. The issue is that `_init_builtin_type_flags` expects container type name in `type_name` variable (`list`, `set`, `dict`...) but for subscripted type it gets `list[str]` etc. Hence, for `BuiltinTypeConstructorObjectType` class I must intercept calling of the method and pass `self.get_container_type()` which ensure that I will call `_init_builtin_type_flags()` with `list` instead of `list[str]`.
